problem.title: Sudoku Solver
problem.description: Write a program to solve a Sudoku puzzle by filling the empty cells ('.'). A valid Sudoku solution must satisfy: each row, each column, and each of the nine 3×3 sub-boxes contains the digits 1-9 without repetition. The given puzzle is guaranteed to have a unique solution.
assumptions: The input puzzle has exactly one valid solution.

example.explanation: All empty cells are filled so that each row, column, and 3x3 box contains digits 1-9 exactly once.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Backtracking
solutions.description: Scan the board for the first empty cell and try placing digits '1' through '9'. For each digit, check whether it is valid in the current row, column, and 3×3 box using boolean sets maintained for efficiency. If valid, place the digit and recurse; if the recursive call returns true the puzzle is solved, otherwise remove the digit (backtrack) and try the next. The worst-case is O(9^m) where m is the number of empty cells, but constraint propagation via the validity check prunes the search tree aggressively.

complexity.time: O(9^m) where m is the number of empty cells; in practice much faster due to pruning
complexity.space: O(1) extra beyond the board (the constraint arrays are fixed size 9×9)

example_walkthrough.text: Find first empty cell (0,2). Try '1': row 0 already has '5','3' — '1' not in row, not in col, not in box → place '1'. Recurse to next empty. If a conflict is eventually found, backtrack: remove digit and try '2', '3', etc. Because there is a unique solution, the search terminates as soon as r==9.
